statement.p: Find the following limits. Enter INF or -INF for \infty or -\infty\text{.} Round answers to at least three decimal places.

li.p: Answer:
Answer:
Answer:
Answer:
Answer:
Answer:
Answer:
Answer:
Answer:
Answer:
The function is continuous at x=2\text{.} So, we can plug 2 in to get e^{-3\cdot 2 + 4} + \sqrt{2} \approx 1.550
Exponentials dominator polynomials. \lim_{x\rightarrow} e^{-6x} 0. So, the whole limit is 0\text{.}
On both sides of x=5\text{,} the function grows to positive infinity as x approaches 8\text{.} So, the limit is \infinity\text{.}
We have two polynomials of equal degres (6). So, the limit is the ratio of the leading coefficients: \frac{8}{-[d2]} = -\frac{8}{[d2]}
Exponentials dominate polynomials. So, we look at e^{-ax}\text{,} which goes to 0\text{.} So, the limit is 0\text{.}
We can simplify the function as e^{(7 - 4)x} = e^{[e3]x}\text{.} As x approaches negative infinity, this approaches 0\text{.}

The numerator has both logaritmic and polynomial elements. Since polynomials dominiate logarithms, we can think of the numerator as behaving like a polynomial of degree 1 at \pm \infty\text{.} The denominator is a higher-degree polynomial. So, it dominates. Thus, the limit is 0\text{.}
The exponent goes to -\infty\text{.} 3^y approaches 0 as y goes to -infty\text{.} So, the limit is 0\text{.}
The exponent goes to 0\text{.} So, the limit is e^0 = 1\text{.}

The numerator dominates since it has higher degree. So, the limit will be positive or negative infinity. We need to determine which. The denominator is led by 3x^2\text{,} which goes to positive infinity as x approaches negative infinity. The numerator is led by -9x^3\text{,} which goes to positive infinity as x approaches negative infinity. So, we have \frac{\text{positive}}{\text{positive}} = \text{positive}\text{.} So, the limit is \infty\text{.}
